## [00:00 - 03:02]

POSTA. This is something that we often don't think about, yet it's the loudest form of body language. I moved to Los Angeles four years ago with my wife and my baby boy and because I didn't know anything about LA, I leased an apartment downtown LA. As an Australian, I thought how cool would it be to live downtown LA? I mean, you see it in the movies, it looks bloody awesome. Boy, was I wrong. I moved into the wrong neighborhood because I was walking one night and had a two-month-old baby boy in a pram and I remember that night I saw a figure from a far and I saw them from a distance and I wasn't even able to see them up close, couldn't see their face or their clothing but I knew just from their posture that something was wrong, that this person seemed dangerous. Then I immediately said to my wife, I said, look, we should do it, you turn and head back to the apartment and we literally just walked straight back to the apartment. If I asked you right now, how do you hold your posture throughout the day? Do you know? How do you look to others? Do you know? Are you aware? It's funny, isn't it? Posture seems simple but we rarely have awareness of what our posture is like until we review it. One of the most powerful exercises that I did a while ago was I placed the GoPro and plugged it into the power at my desk and then I filmed myself for eight hours as I was sitting there working and it was crazy how terrible my posture was and also filmed myself when I'm on stage and then I watched it back and then it made me realize my terrible posture when I was on stage. I was communicating a lack of confidence. Luckily the optimal posture isn't difficult to achieve. Let me take you through what my theater teachers taught me. Number one, I want you to find a wall in your room right now and do this as you listen to me speak. Okay so once you find the wall what you want to do is make sure that your heels, your buttocks and your shoulder blades and your head are all in contact with the wall. Make sure you do that. Now one thing to be careful of is people usually have their pelvis moved forward which means that there's a huge arch and there's a huge gap in the lower back. Try to to tilt your pelvis backwards so that you lessen the gap in your lower back. Then just practice walking away from the wall slightly and then as you walk away from the wall slightly release your head and also make sure your knees are slightly bent. Don't lock your knees. This is the optimal posture to be in. If there was a default posture this would be it. Now one of the biggest problems and 80% of my students suffer from this is rounded shoulders and this happens as a result of us sitting in front of computers all day and if you find this happens to you here's a great exercise that helps you get rid of it. It's called the wall glide. So the wall glide looks like this. You stand right next to the wall and make sure you hold your hands up like this

## [03:02 - 06:04]

and then you glide your hands up and down the wall like this and try to have your elbows touch the wall and if your elbows can't touch the wall that's okay but if you do about 25 of these every morning and every night it's going to help you fix the rounded shoulder problem. This is a problem that I had because I sit in front of the computer a lot for work and then I sit there for recreation as well. I love playing games on the computer. This was such a bad problem for me that I actually had to do rowing so I had to sit on a rowing machine for 15 minutes a day just to row because that really helps you fix the rounded shoulders as well. That's where the problem generally lies with most people. It's in the shoulders. So look now you know a very simple technique to get into the optimal posture and just bear in mind shoulders, knees slightly bent and shoulders pulled back. The second technique to help you with your posture. This is called a grounding technique and you do need to see my feet. So what you want to do is you want to go on your toes and then count to five. So go on your toes and you count one two three four five then you go on your heels very slowly be careful don't fall over and count one two three four five and then go on the outer blades of your feet and then count one two three four five and then you kind of go on the insides of your foot like this like you need to go pee then one two three four five and you jump and this grounds you. You know this is something that I used to do a lot when I felt nervous before I went on stage because when I didn't ground myself I'd go on stage and then I'd keep pacing. I'd keep pacing back and forth because I wasn't grounded or sometimes I find myself on stage and when I don't do the grounding technique to really ground myself sometimes I'll talk and then I'll just almost fall over because I just didn't ground myself. So this is a wonderful little technique you can do just before you're about to go on stage. Obviously don't do this when people can see you you know if you're standing by the side of the stage you don't want to be like I'll be there in a bit mate just kind of you know obviously don't do that it would come across a bit a little bit strange. The third thing I'm going to talk about is your head. Because we look at our phones on a daily basis maybe an hourly basis and because we sit in front of the computer screens every day what happens is we often jut our heads forward you know especially when we're looking at our phone we jut our heads forward like this and then we sit in front of the computer screen we roll our shoulders and then we jut our heads forward like so. This is terrible for your neck and it's also it doesn't look good when it comes to posture. What you want to do is you want to bring your shoulders back and your head you want it to align with your shoulders. Now look I am not a physiotherapist and I'm not a doctor so I don't want to give you advice on this I want to share with you a link on YouTube where you can learn this from one of the best. So if you find yourself jutting your head forward like so check out this video as you build awareness because this video is fantastic to help you remedy this problem. ## [06:04 - 07:04]

video what was your posture like was it optimal do you need to improve something does it seem like you locked your knees were you too stiff and straight or were you slouching start paying attention to your posture because it's the loudest form of body language and it communicates a lot even before you speak even before people can see you clearly they're making judgments on you based on your posture something that my theater teachers used to always say and it's a wonderful tip to conclude this video on and it's that every single time you walk through a door frame the moment you walk through a door frame just kind of anchor that as you need to adjust your posture so for me every time I walk past the door frame I kind of unconsciously fix my posture to ensure that I have the optimal posture and that I'm coming across as being confident.
